course_title: Plants and Archaeology: Food, fuel, foraging and farming in the human past
course_description:
Whether as food, fuel or shelter, plants are among the fundamental building blocks of human life, and archaeology is increasingly able to reconstruct the global, deep-time history of human plant use from the evolution of our earliest ancestors. In this course, students will explore the entangled history of plants and people, including the role of plant foods and fire in human evolution, the global origins and development of farming, the central role of plants in trade and globalisation, and the history of landscape management, biodiversity loss and the emergence of the Anthropocene. Practical classes will introduce students to the methods of archaeobotany, the science that underpins these discoveries, including how plant remains can be routinely recovered from archaeological sites. Our course concludes with a consideration of how archaeological knowledge of the past is helping to revive cultures, improve economic resilience and rebuild landscapes in the present.

Plants have been a fundamentally important resource for human societies throughout our history and archaeobotany (also known as paleoethnobotany) provides a powerful means of understanding plant use and its contribution to human culture change. In this course we will provide an overview of human plant use and the means by which that history has been developed over the sequence of lectures and practical classes.

ARCS2080 combines online modules and weekly practical classes.

Lecture content is presented in a series of interactive modules in UQ Extend that each explore a key theme in archaeobotany and discuss how empirical research has helped to understand each theme. Online content is important for grounding the practical sessions and will be tested in the assessments.

Workshops and practical classes explore key analytical approaches through the exploration of archaeological and modern reference specimens. Practical learning starts in Module A by considering the diversity of plant remains and how we recover them. Modules B and C look at how we analyse different types of plant fossils and Module D looks at flotation and extraction of plant remains at the ATARC.

Workshop/Practical attendance is required.

Assessment tests your knowledge of Modules A, B and C through 6 short quizzes taken in person at the start of scheduled classes in weeks 4-9. The second assessment is a report in which you will provide advice to archaeologists on how to effectively sample and analyse plant remains in a project from Queensland. The report builds on all teaching elements of the course and is an authentic (work integrated learning) assessment.

course_aims: The course aims to develop an understanding of the archaeology of plant use, including providing an overview of how plants have been used in the human career and introducing the method and practice of archaeobotany as an analytical discipline.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand the general principles, methods and theory of archaeobotany
Apply the analytical and theoretical approaches commonly used in archaeobotany to address research questions and understand human-plant interaction in the past
Demonstrate critical understanding of the major methodological issues in archaeobotany and their impact on interpretation
Understand the broader significance of archaeobotanical data to major archaeological and transdisciplinary questions and their appropriate application in these contexts

assessment_summary.title: Practical approaches in archaeobotany
assessment_summary.due_date: 16/03/2026 - 27/04/2026; Quizzes are completed in the first 30 minutes of the scheduled practical class in each week from weeks 4-9 of semester. Quizzes will be completed and submitted in class under exam conditions (closed book and no conferral allowed)

assessment_summary.title: ARCS2080 Report: Archaeobotany methodology statement and recommendation

assessment_details.title: Practical approaches in archaeobotany
assessment_details.due_date: 16/03/2026 - 27/04/2026 Quizzes are completed in the first 30 minutes of the scheduled practical class in each week from weeks 4-9 of semester. Quizzes will be completed and submitted in class under exam conditions (closed book and no conferral allowed)
assessment_details.task_description: This progressive assessment tests your understanding of the practical classes on plant macrofossils and microfossils, including the techniques applied in their analysis and interpretation. The assessment consists of 6 (six) quizzes that are completed weekly and in-person in the first 30 minutes of practical classes during weeks 4-9 of ARCS2080. Each quiz tests knowledge sequentially from the preceding practical class and associated learning (relevant online module and reading). For example the week 6 quiz will test the content of week 5's practical class and relevant case studies and knowledge from that week's lecture and resources from the reading list. Quizzes will be completed in-person during class time using pencils/pens using paper. You must bring your own pens/pencils to class. Each quiz consists of a series of short answer, multiple-choice and other multiform questions. You will not be asked to complete complex calculations but rather answer specific methodological questions and interpret datasets. You will have 30 minutes to complete each quiz. Your 50% mark will be calculated using the top 4 scores from the 6 weekly quizzes. This assessment comprises the secure (identity verified) assessment requirement for ARCS2080 in line with UQ assessment policy. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You will complete and submit the quiz paper in class during the specified time.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Your 50% mark will be calculated using the top 4 scores from the 6 weekly quizzes.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.title: ARCS2080 Report: Archaeobotany methodology statement and recommendation
assessment_details.other_conditions: Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assignment tests the knowledge gained throughout ARCS2080 by applying it to a scenario-based project in which you will prepare a report for an Indigenous partner organisation. The report will present a recommendation identifying which archaeobotany methods should be used in a range of archaeological sites in a remote region of Australia to collect information about past societies and cultural change there. The report requires you to use comparative studies (cited peer-review papers) to justify your recommendations, and will evaluate the potential of the methods studied in ARCS2080 to provide information about the past in sites that have varying preservation and study potential. The partner organisation is particularly interested in understanding whether there is evidence for farming on their Country (covered in weeks 9-12 of the course) and how the research might help to improve management of Country in the future (week 13). The report will be explained, answered and marked using the Inspera Assessment platform via a link in the Assessments folder for this course found in Blackboard: Find more information about using Inspera Assessment from the Library website , which provides the latest information for students about using Inspera. To see what the interface looks like, please try out the Inspera demonstration test here . This shows you the assessment interface using some test questions. Remember, Inspera is a university wide-platform, so there may be some question types in the demo that are not relevant to your discipline. To complete the assessment, you will require access to laptop/desktop computer that meets the device requirements for Inspera assessments. It is important that you check that your laptop meets the device requirements for using the Inspera Assessment platform. If you do not own a suitable laptop, you can borrow one from the Library or book use of a desktop computer in the library . The report will follow a specified structure, as detailed in Inspera. Answers should be in standard English prose, use appropriate disciplinary language and conventions, including full in-text citations and supporting references using APA style, and submitted via Inspera by the specified date and time. You may use tables and charts as required by the task, each with a clearly numbered caption. A reference list will be included. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Classes will discuss the appropriate use of AI in this assessment, should you wish to use it. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub . Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You must submit your assignment electronically by the due time, on the due date.
Your assignment will be prepared and submitted using Inspera, which will be accessed via the link in the Blackboard Ultra Assessment folder. All assessments must be submitted via Inspera and no e-mailed submissions of assessments will be accepted.
By submitting your assessment through Inspera you confirm that all work is your own, except where properly referenced. Only submit assessments that contain your original work.
You are required to retain proof of submission of your assessment. Your Digital Receipt is available for download from your Assignment Dashboard. If you cannot see your submission and download your digital receipt within a few hours, your assessment has not been successfully submitted; please submit again.
If you are experiencing technical difficulties with Blackboard, please contact the ITS Support Team .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Please note that from Semester 2, 2025 the Assessment Procedure has changed. You must submit your application on or before the assessment due date and time.
Grounds of an Extension
A list of acceptable reasons for an extension and the evidence you must provide can be found here . Your request may be refused if you do not meet the acceptable reasons for an extension. All extension requests must be accompanied by documentation that supports the reason for the request. The student submitting the request is fully responsible for all supporting documentation that is provided with the request and should ensure all documents are authentic.
Students registered with Student Support and Wellbeing Services / SAP
In the School of Social Science, extensions granted on the basis of registration with Student Support and Wellbeing Services (SSWS) or a Student Access Plan (SAP) will be the lesser of 7 calendar days or the maximum extension allowable for the assessment task.
More information is provided in Part D of the Assessment Procedure.
Discretionary Extensions
You are eligible for a discretionary extension for a single assessment task per semester of study for a duration of 48 hours, with no documentation required. You can apply if the assessment task allows (see the course profile for details) and if it is your first extension request for that assessment.
You cannot receive an extension using both discretionary grounds and SAP grounds for the same assessment and must select one basis when making their initial request.
Maximum Extension Requests
You can request a maximum of 3 extensions for the same assessment task. If a third extension is necessary, you must submit an Assessment Management Plan in addition to your supporting documentation with your request. If after the third approved extension you submit the assessment late, late penalties will apply.
Late Extension Applications
Late applications must include evidence of the reasons for the late request, detailing why you were unable to apply for an extension by the due date and time. The School of Social Science will not accept personal statements.
Additional information
Extension requests are processed and managed by the School of Social Science Administration Team. Course Coordinators are unable to grant extensions directly.
Extensions in your final semester of study could delay your graduation to the next graduation period.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Assessments will not be accepted if it is more than one week (7 calendar days) late without prior approval.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Why study plants in archaeology? ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This module introduces the scope, origins, history and importance of archaeobotany, providing the intellectual foundation for the course. Watch before class in Week 1. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04

Welcome to Plants and Archaeology This foundation lab session introduces the course, its aims, assessment and structure, building on the online module to explore why you should care about plants and the human past as archaeologists in training. Includes workshop activities on "what are plants?" and "why are plants important?" and a class Q&A to resolve any uncertainties about the course. We also introduce our study area for Assessment 2, which you will prepare for during the whole course. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Plants as food: Preservation and identification ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plant foods are a vital part of life, but hard to study in the past as their physical remains often are not preserved very well. After introducing what foods are, week explore how we can reconstruct the history of plant foods using archaeobotanical tools and a range of artefact and scientific approaches. As well as considering the variety of evidence, we consider how formation processes affect which foods we find, but also how taphonomy - the study of preservation - allows us to predict what we will find in specific environments and sites, empowering us to reconstruct food histories. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Module A: The diversity of plant remains In class we explore the types of plant remains found in archaeological sites, macrofossils and mincrofossils, and find out when and why they are preserved. This session introduces the main microscopy methods used in the practical classes and also the scientific standards expected in our work. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Plant foods and human evolution ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we dig deep to explore how plant foods shaped human evolution, including the development of Homo sapiens and our huge brains. Forget all that nonsense about 'man the hunter' and find out how plant-based nutrition has been a part of our lives for millennia and was essential in making us human. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Module B, Plant Macrofossils. Practical 1: The wonderful world of seeds! This week we explore the wonders of seeds, the most commonly analysed and significant plant macrofossils in archaeobotany. We will look at how they are identified, including understanding the significance of landmarks and standard orientations in our work, and consider some of the challenges of quantification. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Altered states: plants as medicines & intoxicants ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: When did medicines and intoxicants such as drugs and alcohol appear in the human career, how do we know and what effect did they have on human culture change? Simple questions, but ones that have challenged archaeobotanists to develop new techniques and radically change our understanding of an important aspect of human life. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Module B, Plant Macrofossils. Practical 2: Crop remains in archaeobotany Crop remains are fundamentally important for understanding ancient economies and the history of agriculture across the world. We look into the seeds, chaff, straw and other elements that are preserved and discuss how assemblage analysis has transformed our understanding of past farming systems. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Plant trade, exchange and ancient globalisation ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plants have been widely traded for millennia and that trade drove significant cultural, economic and social transformations in the deep past. In this lecture we look at how archaeology can reconstruct plant trade and some key studies of its history from the origins of agriculture and sedentism, to the origins of states, globalisation and European colonialism. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Module B, Plant Macrofossils. Practical 3: Fruits and nuts This week we look at the diversity of fruit, nut and wild seed structures we find in archaeology and explore further how we can use measurements and simple statistics to understand their identity and use. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Fuels and landscape management ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: As the main source of fuels for heat, cooking and industry, woodland and forests have been a major focus for landscape modification and management, and we look at how fuel histories can be constructed and what they tell us in a story now extending into the Pleistocene. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Module B, Plant Macrofossils. Practical 4: Indeterminate plant remains (wood and USO's) Indeterminate plant remains are fragments of larger plant structures, such as woody stems, that lack clear morphology and can't be resolved into whole items, as is the case with seeds. This week introduces the largely anatomical approaches we use to identify them, including wood charcoals and underground storage organs (think potatoes). Using wood charcoals as an example we also explore how to analyse indeterminate remains to reconstruct plant economies and landscape change. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Plants in ancient architecture, crafts and industry ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plant products, including wood, fibres, waxes and stems have been used to produce everything from buildings and artefacts to clothing and ornaments. This week we survey the history of plants beyond food and intoxicants from the Pleistocene to modern world. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Module C, Plant microfossils. Practical 1: Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Gathering for a living ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we start our exploration into the diverse ways humans have sustained themselves. We begin with foraging, also known as hunting and gathering, in which plants are harvested from natural vegetation growth. Sustaining humans since we first evolved, foraging has a deep and diverse history and with ever better methods we have increasingly detailed data from archaeological sites. Food is, however, not just about nutrition, and we also explore how food acquisition and sharing has developed as a key element of social behaviour. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Module C, Plant microfossils. Practical 2: Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Agriculture, cultivation and domestication ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Agriculture is the production of food and other resources from plants (and animals) that are managed and domesticated - meaning they are under human control and selection - and the world's vast population and complex global society is built on it. This week we consider the diversity of agricultural systems and the key concepts cultivation and domestication. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 1: Manual/bucket flotation At UQ's ATARC, we will explore how to extract plant remains via flotation. We begin by considering the aims of such recovery, its logistics and design, learning how to float samples using bucket flotation! This is an active practice in which you will get wet and muddy. See Blackboard for note on appropriate clothing and footwear. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Global perspectives on the origins of agriculture ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Today's global society is built on the ancient and diverse foundations of ancient farming systems. This week we explore how archaeobotany has contributed to explaining where, when and why farming started at diverse places around the world in the deep past. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 2: Machine and hand pump flotation At UQ's ATARC we explore the wonders of machine and hand pump flotation, which allow larger scale processing of soil samples. This is an active practice in which you will get wet and muddy. See Blackboard for note on appropriate clothing and footwear. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Agriculture's ancient diversification and intensification ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Agriculture has undergone significant changes as it spread and developed around the world. This week we look at some important moments of transformation, including the development of mixed farming, intensification, the secondary products revolution and the effects of trade on farming systems in some of the world's earliest examples of globalisation. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 3: Machine and hand pump flotation (continued) We continue and conclude our exploration of machine and hand pump flotation. This is an active practice in which you will get wet and muddy. See Blackboard for note on appropriate clothing and footwear. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Was Australia really a farming bystander? Evaluating Bruce Pascoe's "Dark Emu" ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we look into the issue of how Australia's first people's exploited and shaped the landscape of this continent, including discussing whether they ever developed farming as proposed by Bruce Pascoe in his controversial work "Dark Emu". Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 4: Laboratory recovery and sample curation We explore what is recovered by flotation and recovery rates, what we do next in the lab to extract plant remains, how we approach microfossil recovery and how to curate samples for the future. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Archaeobotany & the challenges of the Anthropocene ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: An understanding of past plant use and landscapes from archaeology is increasingly being used to revitalise and improve resilience of cultures, economies and landscapes today in the face of an unpredictable future. We conclude our course by exploring the potential and future of archaeobotany in the wider world. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Preparing your ARCS2080 Report (Assessment 3) In this workshop we discuss your final assessment and undertake exercises to help complete research for your report. Bring your questions!
